prompt: By the time we arrived at the airport, the flight ______ already departed.

wrongChoiceReasons.B: The present perfect doesn't fit here because the main clause is set in the past.
wrongChoiceReasons.C: The past continuous doesn't show that one past action was completed before another.
wrongChoiceReasons.A: Did is an auxiliary for questions and negatives, not for showing sequence between two past events.

explanation: Great job noticing the sequence! When one past event happens before another, we use the past perfect.
explanationSteps: Identify two past events
The earlier event uses the past perfect
Had + past participle shows completion before the later event
hints: Think about which event happened first
Which tense shows an earlier past action?
Look at the time words carefully — they are the biggest clue to which tense you need.

prompt: Complete the sentence with the correct future perfect form: By 2030, scientists ______ (develop) a cure for the common cold using AI.
explanation: Excellent work with future timelines! The future perfect shows an action completed before a specific future time.
explanationSteps: Identify the future time marker: By 2030
Use will have + past participle
This shows completion before that future point
hints: What tense describes completion before a future moment?
Use will + have + past participle
Look at the time words carefully — they are the biggest clue to which tense you need.

prompt: Rewrite using a perfect tense: 'I started the project. Then the teacher changed the deadline.'
answerKey: I had already started the project when the teacher changed the deadline.
When the teacher changed the deadline, I had already started the project.
explanation: Nice thinking! Combining these events with the past perfect makes the sequence crystal clear.
explanationSteps: Identify the earlier action: starting the project
Use had started for the earlier action
Connect with when for the later action
hints: Which action happened first?
Use had + past participle for the earlier event
Look at the time words carefully — they are the biggest clue to which tense you need.

prompt: The movie was disappointing because I ______ it many times before.

wrongChoiceReasons.B: The simple past doesn't emphasize the earlier experience before another past event.
wrongChoiceReasons.C: The present perfect doesn't fit because the main clause is in the past tense.
wrongChoiceReasons.A: The past continuous describes an ongoing action, not a completed prior experience.

explanation: Well spotted! The past perfect shows that seeing the movie happened before the disappointment in the past.
explanationSteps: The disappointment happened in the past
Seeing the movie happened before that
Use had seen for the earlier past action
hints: Think about the order of past events
Which tense links a prior past experience to a later past situation?
Look at the time words carefully — they are the biggest clue to which tense you need.

prompt: Identify and correct the tense error: 'Climate activists have protested for hours before the law was passed.'
answerKey: Climate activists had protested for hours before the law was passed.
explanation: Good eye! When one past action happens before another past action, the earlier one needs the past perfect.
explanationSteps: The law being passed is in the past
Protesting happened before that
Change have protested to had protested
hints: Compare the timing of protesting and passing the law
The earlier past action needs a different tense
Look at the time words carefully — they are the biggest clue to which tense you need.

prompt: Complete the sentence: By next summer, she ______ (visit) over twenty countries across Europe.
explanation: Fantastic! The future perfect shows that her travels will be completed before next summer arrives.
explanationSteps: By next summer is a future time marker
Use will have + past participle
Visited is the past participle of visit
hints: Which tense shows completion before a future point?
Remember: will + have + past participle
Look at the time words carefully — they are the biggest clue to which tense you need.

prompt: Fill in the two blanks: Students ______ computers for years before schools finally ______ coding classes everywhere.
explanation: Excellent grasp of sequence! The continuous form emphasizes duration, while the simple past marks the later event.
explanationSteps: The first action lasted for years before the second
Use past perfect continuous for duration
Use simple past for the later event
hints: Which blank needs a longer, ongoing action?
The second event happened after the first
Look at the time words carefully — they are the biggest clue to which tense you need.

prompt: Combine into one sentence: 'She finished her homework. Then she watched an educational documentary.'
answerKey: After she had finished her homework, she watched an educational documentary.
She had finished her homework before she watched an educational documentary.
She watched an educational documentary after she had finished her homework.
explanation: Wonderful work connecting the events! Using the past perfect for the earlier action keeps the timeline perfectly clear.
explanationSteps: Identify the earlier action: finishing homework
Use had finished for the earlier action
Use simple past for watching the documentary
hints: Which action came first?
Try using after or before to connect them
Look at the time words carefully — they are the biggest clue to which tense you need.

prompt: If I ______ more money, I would backpack through Southeast Asia next summer.

wrongChoiceReasons.B: The simple present is used in first conditionals, but this sentence uses would for a hypothetical present.
wrongChoiceReasons.C: Would have in the if-clause creates a double conditional structure.
wrongChoiceReasons.A: Had had is past perfect, which belongs in third conditionals for past hypotheticals.

explanation: Nicely done! The second conditional uses the simple past in the if-clause to describe an unreal present situation.
explanationSteps: This is a hypothetical present situation
Second conditional: if + simple past
Main clause uses would + base verb
hints: What type of conditional talks about unreal present situations?
In the if-clause, use simple past for second conditional
Ask yourself: is this a real possibility or an imaginary situation? That tells you which conditional to use.

prompt: Complete the sentence: If the government had invested in renewable energy earlier, pollution levels ______ (drop) significantly by now.
explanation: Great thinking! The third conditional reflects on a past decision that didn't happen and imagines a different present result.
explanationSteps: The if-clause uses past perfect for the unreal past
The main clause uses would have + past participle
This shows a hypothetical past causing a hypothetical present result
hints: Which conditional looks back at past decisions?
Use would + have + past participle in the result clause
Ask yourself: is this a real possibility or an imaginary situation? That tells you which conditional to use.

prompt: Rewrite as a mixed conditional: 'I didn't save my files yesterday. My computer crashed. I lost everything.'
answerKey: If I had saved my files yesterday, I wouldn't have lost everything when my computer crashed.
If I had saved my files yesterday, I wouldn't have lost everything.
explanation: Excellent transformation! You connected a past cause with a past result using the third conditional structure.
explanationSteps: Identify the past mistake: not saving files
Use if + had saved for the unreal past
Use wouldn't have lost for the imagined result
hints: Which action in the past caused the problem?
Use had + past participle in the if-clause
Ask yourself: is this a real possibility or an imaginary situation? That tells you which conditional to use.

prompt: If students ______ access to better libraries, they would read more academic books.

wrongChoiceReasons.B: The simple present fits first conditionals, but this sentence describes an unreal present situation with would.
wrongChoiceReasons.C: Using would in the if-clause is not standard for second conditionals.
wrongChoiceReasons.A: Past perfect is used for third conditionals about the past, not present hypotheticals.

explanation: Well chosen! The second conditional pairs the simple past in the if-clause with would in the main clause for present unreal situations.
explanationSteps: This describes an unreal present situation
Second conditional uses if + simple past
Main clause uses would + base verb
hints: Is this a real or hypothetical situation?
For present hypotheticals, use simple past in the if-clause
Ask yourself: is this a real possibility or an imaginary situation? That tells you which conditional to use.

prompt: Correct the conditional error: 'If the director would have planned the film better, it had been a success.'
answerKey: If the director had planned the film better, it would have been a success.
explanation: Nice catch! Third conditionals use had in the if-clause and would have in the main clause—no would in the if-clause needed.
explanationSteps: Remove would from the if-clause
Use had planned instead of would have planned
Use would have been instead of had been in the main clause
hints: Would doesn't belong in the if-clause of a third conditional
The main clause needs would have + past participle
Ask yourself: is this a real possibility or an imaginary situation? That tells you which conditional to use.

prompt: Fill in the two blanks: If I ______ learned Spanish at school, I ______ be able to travel through Latin America more easily now.
explanation: Perfect! This mixed conditional connects a past unreal situation with a present result.
explanationSteps: Not learning Spanish happened in the past
Use had + past participle for the past unreal condition
Use would + base verb for the present result
hints: The first blank refers to a past unreal condition
The second blank shows a present hypothetical result
Ask yourself: is this a real possibility or an imaginary situation? That tells you which conditional to use.

prompt: Complete the sentence: If teenagers spent less time on social media, they ______ (have) more time for face-to-face friendships.
explanation: Exactly right! This second conditional imagines a different present reality and its likely consequence.
explanationSteps: This is an unreal present situation
The if-clause uses simple past: spent
The main clause uses would + base verb: would have
hints: Which conditional describes unreal present situations?
Use would + base verb in the main clause
Ask yourself: is this a real possibility or an imaginary situation? That tells you which conditional to use.

prompt: Rewrite as a mixed conditional: 'She didn't vote in the last election. She regrets it now.'
answerKey: If she had voted in the last election, she wouldn't regret it now.
She wouldn't regret it now if she had voted in the last election.
explanation: Brilliant! You connected her past action with her present feelings using a mixed conditional perfectly.
explanationSteps: The unreal past action: not voting
Use if + had voted for the past condition
Use wouldn't regret for the present result
hints: Which part refers to the past?
Which part describes her current feeling?
Ask yourself: is this a real possibility or an imaginary situation? That tells you which conditional to use.

prompt: All the exams ______ marked by the teachers before the results were announced.

wrongChoiceReasons.B: The present perfect doesn't fit because the sentence refers to a completed past sequence before another past event.
wrongChoiceReasons.C: The past continuous passive doesn't show completion before another past action.
wrongChoiceReasons.A: The present continuous passive is for ongoing present actions, not a completed past sequence.

explanation: Excellent! The past perfect passive shows that the exams were marked before the announcement happened.
explanationSteps: The results being announced is in the past
Marking happened before that
Use had been + past participle for the earlier passive action
hints: Which action happened first?
Use had been + past participle for the earlier passive action
Remember: passive needs a form of "be" plus the past participle of the main verb.

prompt: Complete the sentence: Passwords ______ (should / change) regularly to protect personal data online.
answerKey: should be changed regularly to protect personal data online
explanation: Great job! Modal passives combine a modal verb with be + past participle to give advice about an action.
explanationSteps: This sentence gives advice using should
The passive form needs be + past participle
Combine them: should be changed
hints: Which modal verb gives advice?
The passive structure is modal + be + past participle
Remember: passive needs a form of "be" plus the past participle of the main verb.

prompt: Rewrite in the passive: 'Journalists say that the streaming platform will change its pricing policy.'
answerKey: The streaming platform is said to be going to change its pricing policy.
The streaming platform is said to change its pricing policy.
It is said that the streaming platform will change its pricing policy.
explanation: Wonderful! The passive reporting structure shifts focus from who says it to what is being said.
explanationSteps: Use subject + is said + to-infinitive
Or use It is said that + original clause
This focuses attention on the information itself
hints: Try starting with the subject of the rumor
Use is said to + infinitive
Remember: passive needs a form of "be" plus the past participle of the main verb.

prompt: Correct the passive voice error: 'The flight tickets had been book weeks before the trip started.'
answerKey: The flight tickets had been booked weeks before the trip started.
explanation: Good eye! The past perfect passive needs the past participle form of the verb.
explanationSteps: Had been shows the past perfect passive
Book needs to become booked
This shows the tickets were reserved before the trip
hints: What is the past participle of book?
Passive voice always uses be + past participle
Remember: passive needs a form of "be" plus the past participle of the main verb.

prompt: Fill in the two blanks: Plastic waste ______ reduced if stricter laws ______ introduced by the government.
explanation: Excellent! This combines a modal passive with a second conditional to discuss possible solutions.
explanationSteps: The sentence proposes a hypothetical solution
Use could be + past participle for modal passive
Use were introduced for the passive conditional clause
hints: The first blank needs a modal passive structure
The second blank is in a hypothetical if-clause
Remember: passive needs a form of "be" plus the past participle of the main verb.

prompt: Students ______ to be among the most motivated learners in the school.

wrongChoiceReasons.B: The active voice here doesn't match the reporting structure needed for general opinion.
wrongChoiceReasons.C: The present continuous active doesn't create the passive reporting meaning.
wrongChoiceReasons.D: The simple past active doesn't express current general belief or reporting.

explanation: Nicely done! 'Are said' is the passive reporting structure used to share what people generally believe.
explanationSteps: This reports general opinion about students
Use be + said in passive reporting
Follow with to + infinitive or a that-clause
hints: Who is expressing this opinion?
The passive form is used when we don't need to name the source
Remember: passive needs a form of "be" plus the past participle of the main verb.

prompt: Complete the sentence: By the time the update was released, the software ______ (already / test) by thousands of beta users.
explanation: Fantastic! The past perfect passive shows that testing was completed before the release happened.
explanationSteps: The release happened in the past
Testing was completed before that
Use had been + past participle for the earlier passive action
hints: Which action happened first?
Use had been + past participle in the passive voice
Remember: passive needs a form of "be" plus the past participle of the main verb.

prompt: She's not answering her phone, so she ______ still be on the plane.

wrongChoiceReasons.B: Should expresses expectation or obligation, not strong deduction about a present situation.
wrongChoiceReasons.C: Can is used for general possibility or ability, not strong logical deduction in affirmative statements.
wrongChoiceReasons.A: Would expresses hypothetical situations, not logical conclusions about present facts.

explanation: Well reasoned! Must is used when we are fairly certain about something based on available evidence.
explanationSteps: The evidence: she's not answering her phone
Must shows strong logical deduction
This means we believe she is very likely on the plane
hints: How certain are you based on the evidence?
Which modal expresses strong logical deduction?
Modal verbs never change form and are always followed directly by the base verb.

prompt: Complete the sentence: Advances in artificial intelligence ______ (may / lead) to major changes in how we work within the next decade.
explanation: Excellent! May, might, and could all express possibility about future technological developments.
explanationSteps: This sentence talks about future possibility
Use may/might/could + base verb
These modals show uncertainty about the future
hints: Is this a certain fact or a possibility?
Use a modal that expresses future possibility
Modal verbs never change form and are always followed directly by the base verb.

prompt: Rewrite using a modal verb for advice: 'It is a good idea to review your notes before the exam.'
answerKey: You should review your notes before the exam.
You ought to review your notes before the exam.
You had better review your notes before the exam.
explanation: Great transformation! Should and ought to are perfect for giving friendly, sensible advice.
explanationSteps: Identify that this is giving advice
Should + base verb gives strong advice
Ought to + base verb is a slightly more formal alternative
hints: Which modal verbs are commonly used for advice?
Try starting with 'You should...'
Modal verbs never change form and are always followed directly by the base verb.

prompt: Correct the modal verb error: 'The increase in crime must due to economic factors, according to experts.'
answerKey: The increase in crime must be due to economic factors, according to experts.
explanation: Good catch! Must always needs a main verb or be after it to form a complete verb phrase.
explanationSteps: Must is a modal verb
It must be followed by a base verb or the verb be
Add be after must to complete the phrase
hints: What form of the verb follows modal verbs?
Modal verbs need the base form of be
Modal verbs never change form and are always followed directly by the base verb.

prompt: Complete the sentence: Streaming services ______ (may / become) more expensive if production costs continue to rise.
explanation: Excellent! May, might, and could all express that this is one possible future outcome.
explanationSteps: This is a future possibility, not a certainty
Use may/might/could + base verb
Become stays in its base form after the modal
hints: Is this a prediction or a possibility?
Modal verbs are followed by the base form of the verb
Modal verbs never change form and are always followed directly by the base verb.

prompt: Complete the sentence: You ______ (should / check) the visa requirements before booking international flights.
explanation: Perfect! This modal gives practical advice to travelers planning a trip abroad.
explanationSteps: This sentence gives travel advice
Should + base verb is the most common advice modal
Ought to and had better are also acceptable
hints: Which modal is commonly used for giving advice?
The base form of the verb follows the modal
Modal verbs never change form and are always followed directly by the base verb.

prompt: The app ______ allows offline messaging is extremely popular among teenagers.

wrongChoiceReasons.A: Which can be used for things, but in defining relative clauses, that is often preferred, especially in everyday English.
wrongChoiceReasons.C: Who is used for people, not for apps or things.
wrongChoiceReasons.D: Whose shows possession and would need to be followed by a noun, which doesn't fit here.

explanation: Well chosen! That is commonly used in defining relative clauses to identify a specific thing.
explanationSteps: This is a defining relative clause identifying the app
That refers to things
The clause tells us which app is meant
hints: Is this clause essential to the meaning?
Which relative pronoun refers to things in defining clauses?
Who is for people, which is for things, and where is for places — choose the pronoun that matches the noun.

prompt: Complete the sentence with a non-defining relative clause: My physics teacher, ______ (graduate / MIT), explained quantum computing last week.
explanation: Wonderful! Non-defining clauses add extra information and always use commas.
explanationSteps: This adds extra information about the teacher
Use who for people
Remember the commas around the non-defining clause
hints: Does this clause identify the teacher or add extra information?
Use who for people in non-defining clauses
Who is for people, which is for things, and where is for places — choose the pronoun that matches the noun.

prompt: Rewrite using a reduced relative clause: 'The tourists who were waiting for the delayed flight looked exhausted.'
answerKey: The tourists waiting for the delayed flight looked exhausted.
explanation: Excellent! Removing who were creates a more concise reduced relative clause without changing the meaning.
explanationSteps: Identify the relative clause: who were waiting
Remove the relative pronoun and the verb be
Keep the present participle waiting
hints: Which words can you remove without changing the meaning?
Reduced relative clauses drop who + be
Who is for people, which is for things, and where is for places — choose the pronoun that matches the noun.

prompt: Correct the relative clause error: 'The documentary what won the award revealed important truths about social media.'
answerKey: The documentary that won the award revealed important truths about social media.
explanation: Good eye! What is not used as a relative pronoun in standard relative clauses for things.
explanationSteps: What is not used as a relative pronoun here
Use that or which for things
That is preferred in defining relative clauses
hints: Which relative pronouns refer to things?
What is not used in standard relative clauses
Who is for people, which is for things, and where is for places — choose the pronoun that matches the noun.

prompt: Fill in the two blanks: Climate change, ______ affects millions of people, is a problem ______ requires immediate global action.
explanation: Perfect! The first adds extra information with a non-defining clause, while the second defines the problem with a defining clause.
explanationSteps: The first clause is non-defining and uses commas
Use which for the non-defining clause
Use that or which for the defining clause
hints: Which clause has commas around it?
Non-defining clauses use which; defining clauses can use that or which
Who is for people, which is for things, and where is for places — choose the pronoun that matches the noun.

prompt: Complete the sentence with a reduced relative clause: The algorithm ______ (design / improve) recommendations is constantly updated.
explanation: Excellent! Designed to improve is a neat reduced relative clause that modifies the algorithm.
explanationSteps: The full clause would be which was designed
Remove which was to reduce it
Designed to improve acts as an adjective phrase
hints: What is the past participle of design?
Try removing the relative pronoun and was
Who is for people, which is for things, and where is for places — choose the pronoun that matches the noun.

prompt: 'I have never visited Asia before,' Tom said. Tom said that he ______ never visited Asia before.

wrongChoiceReasons.A: The present perfect doesn't shift back in reported speech when the main verb is in the past tense.
wrongChoiceReasons.C: Have doesn't agree with the subject and doesn't show the required tense backshift.
wrongChoiceReasons.D: Would have changes the meaning to conditional, which doesn't match the original statement.

explanation: Great work! In reported speech, present perfect shifts back to past perfect when the reporting verb is in the past tense.
explanationSteps: The original statement uses present perfect
The reporting verb said is in the past
Shift present perfect to past perfect: have visited becomes had visited
hints: What tense is the reporting verb?
When the reporting verb is past, present perfect usually shifts back
Shift everything one step back: present becomes past, and pronouns shift to match the reporter.

prompt: Complete the reported question: 'Are smartphones replacing laptops?' The journalist asked ______ smartphones were replacing laptops.
explanation: Perfect! When reporting yes/no questions, we use if or whether to introduce the reported clause.
explanationSteps: This is a yes/no question
In reported speech, use if or whether
The word order changes from question to statement form
hints: Is this a wh-question or a yes/no question?
Use if or whether for reported yes/no questions
Shift everything one step back: present becomes past, and pronouns shift to match the reporter.

prompt: Rewrite in reported speech: 'The exam will be postponed,' the principal announced.
answerKey: The principal announced that the exam would be postponed.
The principal announced the exam would be postponed.
explanation: Wonderful! You correctly shifted will to would when reporting in the past tense.
explanationSteps: The reporting verb announced is in the past
Will changes to would in reported speech
Be postponed stays in the passive form
hints: What happens to will when the reporting verb is past?
Change will to would
Shift everything one step back: present becomes past, and pronouns shift to match the reporter.

prompt: Correct the reported speech error: 'The series is cancelled,' the network stated. The network stated that the series is cancelled.
answerKey: The network stated that the series was cancelled.
explanation: Good catch! When the reporting verb is in the past tense, the present tense in the original statement needs to shift back.
explanationSteps: The reporting verb stated is past tense
The original uses present tense is
Shift is to was in reported speech
hints: What tense is stated?
Present tense shifts to past tense in reported speech
Shift everything one step back: present becomes past, and pronouns shift to match the reporter.

prompt: Fill in the two blanks: 'Why are young people protesting?' The reporter asked ______ young people ______ protesting.
explanation: Excellent! Wh-questions keep their question word, and the verb shifts back to the past tense.
explanationSteps: Keep the wh- word why
Change the question word order to statement word order
Shift are to were because asked is past tense
hints: Which word introduces the reported question?
The original verb is present tense; shift it back
Shift everything one step back: present becomes past, and pronouns shift to match the reporter.

prompt: Teenagers spend ______ too much time on screens ______ not enough time reading physical books.

wrongChoiceReasons.B: Neither...nor creates a double negative meaning, which doesn't fit this contrast between two problematic habits.
wrongChoiceReasons.C: Both...and would connect two positive ideas, but this sentence contrasts two negative situations.
wrongChoiceReasons.D: Not only...but also adds emphasis to two related ideas, but the sentence presents a balanced contrast rather than emphasis.

explanation: Well done! Either...or presents two alternatives or problems side by side in a balanced way.
explanationSteps: The sentence presents two problems
Either...or shows a choice or balance between two options
This fits the contrast between screen time and reading time
hints: Are the two ideas connected by addition or alternative?
Which correlative conjunction presents alternatives?
Look at the relationship between the two ideas — do they add, contrast, explain, or show a result?

prompt: Complete the sentence: The school introduced digital textbooks; ______ , students still preferred printed books for revision.
explanation: Excellent! However shows a clear contrast between the school's action and the students' preference.
explanationSteps: The first idea: digital textbooks were introduced
The second idea: students still preferred printed books
However shows contrast between these two ideas
hints: Is the second idea similar to or different from the first?
Use a conjunctive adverb that shows contrast
Look at the relationship between the two ideas — do they add, contrast, explain, or show a result?

prompt: Rewrite using correlative conjunctions: 'You can stay in a hotel. You can camp in the mountains.'
answerKey: You can either stay in a hotel or camp in the mountains.
Either you can stay in a hotel, or you can camp in the mountains.
explanation: Great transformation! Either...or neatly presents the two travel accommodation options.
explanationSteps: There are two options presented
Use either before the first option
Use or before the second option
hints: Which correlative conjunction presents a choice between two options?
Place either before the first option and or before the second
Look at the relationship between the two ideas — do they add, contrast, explain, or show a result?

prompt: Correct the connector error: 'The film received poor reviews, therefore it became a box office hit.'
answerKey: The film received poor reviews; however, it became a box office hit.
explanation: Nice catch! Therefore shows a result, but these two ideas are actually in contrast with each other.
explanationSteps: Poor reviews and box office success contrast
Therefore indicates cause and effect
Use however, but, or yet to show contrast instead
hints: Do poor reviews usually lead to box office success?
Choose a connector that shows contrast rather than result
Look at the relationship between the two ideas — do they add, contrast, explain, or show a result?

prompt: Fill in the two blanks: ______ governments ______ citizens must work together to reduce plastic pollution.
explanation: Perfect! Both...and emphasizes that two groups share equal responsibility for solving the problem.
explanationSteps: Two subjects share responsibility
Use both before the first subject
Use and before the second subject
hints: Are both groups needed for the solution?
Which correlative conjunction adds two subjects together?
Look at the relationship between the two ideas — do they add, contrast, explain, or show a result?

prompt: The team of students ______ preparing for the national debate competition.

wrongChoiceReasons.B: Although team members are plural, the collective noun team itself is grammatically singular in standard usage.
wrongChoiceReasons.C: Were is plural and past tense, but the sentence describes a present situation with a singular collective subject.
wrongChoiceReasons.D: Have been is plural and doesn't agree with the singular collective noun team.

explanation: Well chosen! Collective nouns like team usually take a singular verb when the group acts as a single unit.
explanationSteps: Team is a collective noun
When acting as a unit, collective nouns take singular verbs
Use is preparing for the present continuous singular
hints: Is the team acting as one unit or as separate individuals?
Collective nouns usually take singular verbs in standard grammar
Count the subject — if it is one person or thing, the verb usually needs -s or -es.

prompt: Complete the sentence: The list of approved apps ______ (be) updated every semester by the school board.
explanation: Excellent! The subject is list, which is singular, so the verb must also be singular despite the plural noun apps in between.
explanationSteps: Identify the subject: list
Of approved apps is a prepositional phrase, not the subject
Use is because list is singular
hints: What is the real subject of the sentence?
Ignore prepositional phrases when choosing the verb form
Count the subject — if it is one person or thing, the verb usually needs -s or -es.

prompt: Correct the subject-verb agreement error: 'The cast of the series are exceptionally talented.'
answerKey: The cast of the series is exceptionally talented.
explanation: Good eye! Cast is a collective noun that takes a singular verb when referring to the group as a whole.
explanationSteps: Cast is a collective noun
When referring to the group as a unit, use a singular verb
Change are to is
hints: Is cast acting as a single unit or as individuals?
Collective nouns usually take singular verbs in this context
Count the subject — if it is one person or thing, the verb usually needs -s or -es.

prompt: Rewrite with correct subject-verb agreement: 'The luggage of the passengers have been checked by security.'
answerKey: The luggage of the passengers has been checked by security.
explanation: Wonderful! Luggage is an uncountable noun that takes a singular verb, regardless of the plural noun passengers nearby.
explanationSteps: Identify the subject: luggage
Luggage is uncountable and singular
Change have been to has been
hints: Is luggage countable or uncountable?
Uncountable nouns take singular verbs
Count the subject — if it is one person or thing, the verb usually needs -s or -es.

prompt: Fill in the two blanks: The committee ______ divided on whether the new policy ______ effective.
explanation: Perfect! Committee is a collective noun that takes a singular verb, and policy is also singular.
explanationSteps: Committee is a collective noun acting as a unit
Use is for the singular collective noun
Policy is singular, so use is as well
hints: Is committee singular or plural in standard grammar?
Policy is also singular
Count the subject — if it is one person or thing, the verb usually needs -s or -es.

prompt: The backpackers were exhausted because they ______ for twelve hours without a break.

wrongChoiceReasons.B: The present perfect continuous doesn't fit because the main clause sets the scene in the past.
wrongChoiceReasons.C: The past continuous doesn't show duration leading up to another past moment.
wrongChoiceReasons.A: The past perfect simple doesn't emphasise the ongoing nature of the activity.

explanation: Great observation! The past perfect continuous emphasises how long the action lasted before another past event.
explanationSteps: The exhaustion happened in the past
Walking lasted for a period before that
Had been + present participle shows duration up to a past point
hints: Think about how long the action lasted before the exhaustion
Which tense emphasises duration before another past event?
Use had been + -ing for the ongoing earlier action

prompt: Complete the sentence: By the end of this year, AI researchers ______ (work) on this ethical framework for over eighteen months.
explanation: Excellent! The future perfect continuous highlights the ongoing effort that will continue up to a specific future moment.
explanationSteps: By the end of this year is a future time marker
Use will have been + present participle
This shows duration up to that future point
hints: Which tense shows ongoing action up to a future moment?
Use will + have been + -ing
The researchers are still working now

prompt: Combine into one sentence: 'The exam finished. The students realised they had made careless mistakes.'
answerKey: By the time the students realised they had made careless mistakes, the exam had already finished.
The exam had already finished by the time the students realised they had made careless mistakes.
When the students realised they had made careless mistakes, the exam had already finished.
explanation: Nice work! Using the past perfect for the exam finishing makes the sequence of events perfectly clear.
explanationSteps: Identify the earlier action: the exam finishing
Use the past perfect for the earlier action
Connect the later realisation with when or by the time
hints: Which event happened first?
Use had + past participle for the earlier event
Try using by the time to link the two events

prompt: Identify and correct the tense error: 'Online activism has grown rapidly since the movement started, but many people still underestimated its power.'
answerKey: Online activism has grown rapidly since the movement started, but many people still underestimate its power.
explanation: Good eye! The second clause describes a current attitude, so the present tense is more appropriate than the past.
explanationSteps: The first clause describes growth up to now
The second clause describes a current situation
Change underestimated to the present tense underestimate
hints: Does the second clause refer to the past or the present?
Current attitudes need the present tense
Compare the time frames of both clauses

prompt: Fill in the two blanks: By the time the train ______ at the platform, the passengers ______ for over forty minutes in the cold.
explanation: Perfect! The time clause uses the present simple for a future event, while the main clause uses the future perfect continuous for duration.
explanationSteps: By the time introduces a future event
Use present simple in the time clause
Use future perfect continuous for the waiting duration
hints: Which tense is used in time clauses about the future?
Which tense shows duration up to that future moment?
Remember: present simple after by the time

prompt: Complete the sentence: Streaming platforms ______ (compete) for viewers' attention for the past decade, and the battle shows no sign of ending.
explanation: Fantastic! The present perfect continuous captures the ongoing rivalry that started in the past and continues now.
explanationSteps: For the past decade shows duration from past to present
Use have been + present participle
This emphasises the ongoing nature of the competition
hints: Does the competition continue into the present?
Which tense shows an action that started in the past and continues now?
Use have been + -ing

prompt: This time next week, most teenagers ______ their summer coding camp at the tech centre.

wrongChoiceReasons.B: The simple future doesn't emphasise the ongoing nature of an action at a specific future moment.
wrongChoiceReasons.C: The present continuous is for arrangements, but this sentence highlights an action in progress at a future time.
wrongChoiceReasons.D: The future perfect suggests completion, but the sentence focuses on an activity in progress.

explanation: Well chosen! The future continuous describes an action that will be in progress at a specific future time.
explanationSteps: This time next week is a specific future moment
Use will be + present participle
This shows the activity will be ongoing then
hints: Is the sentence about a completed action or one in progress?
Which tense describes an ongoing action at a future moment?
Use will be + -ing

prompt: Rewrite using a perfect tense: 'The teacher explained the grammar rule. Then the students practised it in pairs.'
answerKey: After the teacher had explained the grammar rule, the students practised it in pairs.
The students practised the grammar rule in pairs after the teacher had explained it.
Once the teacher had explained the grammar rule, the students practised it in pairs.
explanation: Wonderful! Using the past perfect for the explanation keeps the classroom timeline crystal clear.
explanationSteps: Identify the earlier action: the teacher's explanation
Use had explained for the earlier action
Use the simple past for the students' practice
hints: Which action happened first?
Use had + past participle for the earlier event
Try using after or once to connect the clauses

prompt: If hostels ______ cheaper in peak season, more students would travel during the summer holidays.

wrongChoiceReasons.B: The simple present is used in first conditionals for real possibilities, but this sentence describes an unreal present.
wrongChoiceReasons.C: Would be in the if-clause creates a double conditional structure, which is non-standard.
wrongChoiceReasons.A: Had been is the past perfect, used in third conditionals for past hypotheticals.

explanation: Nicely done! The second conditional uses the simple past in the if-clause to imagine a different present reality.
explanationSteps: This describes an unreal present situation
Second conditional: if + simple past
Main clause uses would + base verb
hints: Is this a real or hypothetical situation?
Which conditional talks about unreal present situations?
Use the simple past in the if-clause

prompt: Complete the sentence: If local communities had been consulted earlier, the housing crisis ______ (avoid) to a large extent.
explanation: Great thinking! The third conditional looks back at a past decision and imagines a different outcome.
explanationSteps: The if-clause uses past perfect for the unreal past
The main clause uses would have + past participle
The passive voice is needed because the crisis would be avoided
hints: Which conditional reflects on past decisions?
Use would + have + been + past participle for the passive
The main clause needs the passive voice

prompt: Rewrite as a mixed conditional: 'He bought a new smartphone last month. He doesn't have any savings now.'
answerKey: If he hadn't bought a new smartphone last month, he would have some savings now.
He would have some savings now if he hadn't bought a new smartphone last month.
explanation: Excellent! You linked a past purchase with a present financial situation using a mixed conditional.
explanationSteps: Identify the past action: buying the smartphone
Use if + hadn't bought for the unreal past
Use would have for the present result
hints: Which action in the past caused the present situation?
Use had + not + past participle in the if-clause
The result clause describes the present

prompt: Correct the conditional error: 'If schools would provide free tablets, every student could access digital resources.'
answerKey: If schools provided free tablets, every student could access digital resources.
explanation: Nice catch! Second conditionals use the simple past in the if-clause, not would.
explanationSteps: Remove would from the if-clause
Use the simple past provided
Keep could in the main clause
hints: Would does not belong in the if-clause of a second conditional
Use the simple past in the if-clause
Which form of provide is the simple past?

prompt: Fill in the two blanks: If I ______ booked the tickets earlier, I ______ be flying to Tokyo tomorrow instead of staying home.
explanation: Perfect! This mixed conditional connects a past failure with a present hypothetical situation.
explanationSteps: Not booking earlier is a past unreal condition
Use had + past participle for the past unreal
Use would + base verb for the present result
hints: The first blank refers to a past unreal condition
The second blank shows a present hypothetical result
Use had in the if-clause and would in the main clause

prompt: Complete the sentence: If the documentary ______ (broadcast) earlier, it would have reached a much larger audience.
explanation: Great work! The third conditional uses the past perfect passive when the unreal past condition is passive.
explanationSteps: The documentary didn't get broadcast earlier
Use if + had been + past participle for the passive
The main clause uses would have + past participle
hints: Which conditional looks back at the past?
The if-clause needs the passive voice
Use had been + past participle

prompt: ______ the government acted sooner, the protest movement would never have gained such momentum.

wrongChoiceReasons.B: Have is not used to begin inverted conditional structures; the auxiliary had is required.
wrongChoiceReasons.C: If is correct for standard conditionals, but this sentence omits if and inverts the subject and auxiliary.
wrongChoiceReasons.A: Would does not introduce an inverted third conditional clause.

explanation: Well done! Inverted third conditionals drop if and place had before the subject for a more formal tone.
explanationSteps: This is a formal inversion of a third conditional
Remove if and move had to the front
Had the government acted = If the government had acted
hints: Notice that if is missing from the sentence
Which auxiliary verb begins an inverted third conditional?
Move had to the beginning of the clause

prompt: Rewrite using a second conditional: 'Teenagers spend too much time gaming. They don't get enough sleep.'
answerKey: If teenagers didn't spend too much time gaming, they would get enough sleep.
Teenagers would get enough sleep if they didn't spend too much time gaming.
explanation: Brilliant! You turned a present problem into a hypothetical solution using the second conditional.
explanationSteps: Identify the present problem: spending too much time gaming
Use if + didn't spend for the unreal present
Use would get for the imagined result
hints: Which action needs to change in the hypothetical situation?
Use didn't + base verb in the if-clause
Use would + base verb in the main clause

prompt: The school had its computer lab ______ by a team of professional technicians before the new term began.

wrongChoiceReasons.B: Upgrade is the base form, but the causative structure have something done requires the past participle.
wrongChoiceReasons.C: Upgrading is a present participle, which doesn't fit the have something done pattern.
wrongChoiceReasons.D: To upgrade is an infinitive, which is not used in the standard causative passive structure.

explanation: Excellent! Have something done is a causative structure where the past participle shows the action was arranged for someone else.
explanationSteps: Have + object + past participle is the causative pattern
The school arranged for others to upgrade the lab
Use upgraded as the past participle
hints: Which structure shows that someone arranged for an action to be done?
Have something + past participle
What is the past participle of upgrade?

prompt: Complete the sentence: Lost passports ______ (can / replace) at the embassy within five working days.
explanation: Great job! Modal passives combine a modal verb with be + past participle to describe what is possible.
explanationSteps: This sentence describes a possibility using can
The passive form needs be + past participle
Combine them: can be replaced
hints: Which modal verb expresses possibility?
The passive structure is modal + be + past participle
What is the past participle of replace?

prompt: Rewrite in the passive: 'Experts believe that social media algorithms influence political opinions.'
answerKey: Social media algorithms are believed to influence political opinions.
It is believed that social media algorithms influence political opinions.
explanation: Wonderful! The passive reporting structure shifts the focus from the experts to the algorithms themselves.
explanationSteps: Use subject + are believed + to-infinitive
Or use It is believed that + original clause
This focuses attention on the belief rather than the believers
hints: Try starting with the subject of the belief
Use are believed to + infinitive
Alternatively, start with It is believed that

prompt: Correct the passive voice error: 'The new educational app has been download over a million times since its launch.'
answerKey: The new educational app has been downloaded over a million times since its launch.
explanation: Good eye! The present perfect passive needs the past participle form of the verb.
explanationSteps: Has been shows the present perfect passive
Download needs to become downloaded
This shows the completion of the action up to now
hints: What is the past participle of download?
Passive voice always uses be + past participle
Check the spelling of the past participle

prompt: Fill in the two blanks: Fake news ______ stopped more effectively if stricter fact-checking measures ______ introduced by social media platforms.
explanation: Excellent! This combines a modal passive with a second conditional to discuss a possible solution.
explanationSteps: The sentence proposes a hypothetical solution
Use could be + past participle for modal passive
Use were introduced for the passive conditional clause
hints: The first blank needs a modal passive structure
The second blank is in a hypothetical if-clause
Use were for the passive conditional

prompt: Complete the sentence: Several travellers ______ (get / delay) at the border because of visa complications.
explanation: Perfect! Get + past participle is an informal alternative to the standard passive voice, common in spoken English.
explanationSteps: Get can replace be in informal passive constructions
Use got + past participle for the past tense
This describes an event that happened to the travellers
hints: Which informal passive structure uses get instead of be?
Use got + past participle in the past tense
What is the past participle of delay?

prompt: The new exam format ______ to be more inclusive of students with different learning needs.

wrongChoiceReasons.B: The active voice here doesn't match the reporting structure needed for general opinion.
wrongChoiceReasons.C: The present continuous active doesn't create the passive reporting meaning.
wrongChoiceReasons.A: The simple past active doesn't express current general belief or reporting.

explanation: Nicely done! 'Is said' is the passive reporting structure used to share what people generally believe or report.
explanationSteps: This reports general opinion about the exam format
Use be + said in passive reporting
Follow with to + infinitive or a that-clause
hints: Who is expressing this opinion?
The passive form is used when we don't need to name the source
Use is said to be or is said that

prompt: The app keeps crashing, so there ______ be a bug in the latest software update.

wrongChoiceReasons.B: Should expresses expectation or obligation, not strong deduction about a present situation.
wrongChoiceReasons.C: Can is used for general possibility or ability, not strong logical deduction in affirmative statements.
wrongChoiceReasons.A: Would expresses hypothetical situations, not logical conclusions about present facts.

explanation: Well reasoned! Must is used when we draw a logical conclusion from clear evidence.
explanationSteps: The evidence: the app keeps crashing
Must shows strong logical deduction
This means we believe a bug is very likely
hints: How certain are you based on the evidence?
Which modal expresses strong logical deduction?
Must is used when the evidence points strongly to one conclusion

prompt: Complete the sentence: All students ______ (must / submit) their coursework by Friday evening at the latest.
explanation: Excellent! Must and have to express strong obligation, which is perfect for strict deadlines.
explanationSteps: This sentence states a strict rule or deadline
Use must + base verb for strong obligation
Have to is also acceptable for external rules
hints: Is this a suggestion or a strict requirement?
Which modal expresses strong obligation?
The base form of the verb follows the modal

prompt: Rewrite using a modal verb for criticism: 'It was irresponsible of the company to ignore safety warnings.'
answerKey: The company shouldn't have ignored safety warnings.
The company ought not to have ignored safety warnings.
explanation: Great transformation! Shouldn't have is a natural way to criticise a past action.
explanationSteps: Identify that this is criticising a past action
Shouldn't have + past participle criticises past behaviour
This expresses that the company made a mistake
hints: Which modal structure is used to criticise past actions?
Use shouldn't + have + past participle
Try starting with 'The company shouldn't have...'

prompt: Correct the modal verb error: 'We must have checked the gate schedule before we arrived at the airport.'
answerKey: We should have checked the gate schedule before we arrived at the airport.
explanation: Good catch! Must have expresses certainty about the past, but here we are giving advice about a past mistake.
explanationSteps: The sentence criticises a past omission
Should have + past participle gives past advice
Must have changes the meaning to certainty, which doesn't fit
hints: Is the sentence expressing certainty or giving advice about the past?
Use should have for advice about a past mistake
Must have is for strong deduction, not criticism

prompt: Complete the sentence: Independent journalism ______ (may / survive) the shift to digital platforms if readers continue to value quality reporting.
explanation: Excellent! May, might, and could all express that this is one possible future outcome.
explanationSteps: This is a future possibility, not a certainty
Use may/might/could + base verb
Survive stays in its base form after the modal
hints: Is this a prediction or a possibility?
Modal verbs are followed by the base form of the verb
Use may, might, or could for future possibility

prompt: Fill in the two blanks: Personal data ______ (should / protect) more carefully, or users ______ face serious privacy risks.
explanation: Perfect! This combines a modal passive with a modal of possibility to give a warning about data protection.
explanationSteps: The first clause gives advice about data protection
Use should be + past participle for modal passive
Use may for the possible consequence
hints: The first blank needs a modal passive structure
Which modal expresses future possibility?
Use should be protected for the advice clause

prompt: The charity ______ provides free meals to homeless teenagers was founded by a group of university students.

wrongChoiceReasons.A: Which can be used for things, but in defining relative clauses, that is often preferred, especially in everyday English.
wrongChoiceReasons.C: Who is used for people, not for charities or organisations.
wrongChoiceReasons.D: Whose shows possession and would need to be followed by a noun, which doesn't fit here.

explanation: Well chosen! That is commonly used in defining relative clauses to identify a specific organisation.
explanationSteps: This is a defining relative clause identifying the charity
That refers to things and organisations
The clause tells us which charity is meant
hints: Is this clause essential to the meaning?
Which relative pronoun refers to things in defining clauses?
That or which can be used, but that is preferred here

prompt: Complete the sentence with a non-defining relative clause: Our tour guide, ______ (speak / four languages), made the trip much more enjoyable.
explanation: Wonderful! Non-defining clauses add extra information and always use commas.
explanationSteps: This adds extra information about the tour guide
Use who for people
Remember the commas around the non-defining clause
hints: Does this clause identify the guide or add extra information?
Use who for people in non-defining clauses
Place commas before and after the clause

prompt: Rewrite using a reduced relative clause: 'The assignments that were submitted late were marked down by the teacher.'
answerKey: The assignments submitted late were marked down by the teacher.
explanation: Excellent! Removing that were creates a more concise reduced relative clause without changing the meaning.
explanationSteps: Identify the relative clause: that were submitted
Remove the relative pronoun and the verb be
Keep the past participle submitted
hints: Which words can you remove without changing the meaning?
Reduced relative clauses drop that + be
Keep the past participle as an adjective phrase

prompt: Correct the relative clause error: 'The online course where covers AI ethics is extremely popular among students.'
answerKey: The online course that covers AI ethics is extremely popular among students.
explanation: Good eye! Where is used for places, but here we need a relative pronoun that refers to the course itself.
explanationSteps: Where refers to places, not things
Use that or which for things
That is preferred in defining relative clauses
hints: Does the clause refer to a place or the subject of the course?
Use that or which for things
Where is only for locations

prompt: Fill in the two blanks: The documentary series, ______ won several awards, explores issues ______ affect young people today.
explanation: Perfect! The first adds extra information with a non-defining clause, while the second defines the issues with a defining clause.
explanationSteps: The first clause is non-defining and uses commas
Use which for the non-defining clause
Use that or which for the defining clause
hints: Which clause has commas around it?
Non-defining clauses use which; defining clauses can use that or which
The first clause adds extra information

prompt: Complete the sentence with a reduced relative clause: The policies ______ (introduce / last year) to tackle inequality are already showing results.
explanation: Excellent! Introduced last year is a neat reduced relative clause that modifies the policies.
explanationSteps: The full clause would be which were introduced
Remove which were to reduce it
Introduced last year acts as an adjective phrase
hints: What is the past participle of introduce?
Try removing the relative pronoun and were
Keep the past participle and time phrase

prompt: 'We are implementing a new grading system,' the headteacher said. The headteacher said that they ______ implementing a new grading system.

wrongChoiceReasons.B: The present continuous doesn't shift back in reported speech when the reporting verb is in the past tense.
wrongChoiceReasons.C: Had been changes the meaning to past perfect continuous, which is not the required backshift from present continuous.
wrongChoiceReasons.A: Would be changes the meaning to conditional future, which doesn't match the original statement.

explanation: Great work! In reported speech, present continuous shifts back to past continuous when the reporting verb is in the past tense.
explanationSteps: The original statement uses present continuous
The reporting verb said is in the past
Shift present continuous to past continuous: are becomes were
hints: What tense is the reporting verb?
When the reporting verb is past, present continuous usually shifts back
Are becomes were in the past

prompt: Complete the reported question: 'Have the flight times changed?' She asked ______ the flight times had changed.
explanation: Perfect! When reporting yes/no questions, we use if or whether to introduce the reported clause.
explanationSteps: This is a yes/no question in the present perfect
In reported speech, use if or whether
The word order changes from question to statement form
hints: Is this a wh-question or a yes/no question?
Use if or whether for reported yes/no questions
Change the word order to statement form

prompt: Rewrite in reported speech: 'The new feature will be available next month,' the tech company announced.
answerKey: The tech company announced that the new feature would be available the following month.
The tech company announced that the new feature would be available next month.
explanation: Wonderful! You correctly shifted will to would when reporting in the past tense.
explanationSteps: The reporting verb announced is in the past
Will changes to would in reported speech
Be available stays in the same form
hints: What happens to will when the reporting verb is past?
Change will to would
You can also change next month to the following month

prompt: Correct the reported speech error: 'Turn off your phones,' the director said. The director said to turn off our phones.
answerKey: The director told us to turn off our phones.
explanation: Good catch! While said to is sometimes used, told us is the more standard and natural way to report an imperative.
explanationSteps: Imperatives are usually reported with told + object + to-infinitive
Use told us instead of said to
This is more natural for reported commands
hints: Which verb is standard for reporting commands?
Use told + person + to-infinitive
Said to is less common for imperatives

prompt: Fill in the two blanks: 'When will the new policy take effect?' The citizens asked ______ the new policy ______ take effect.
explanation: Excellent! Wh-questions keep their question word, and the future tense shifts back to the conditional.
explanationSteps: Keep the wh- word when
Change the question word order to statement word order
Shift will to would because asked is past tense
hints: Which word introduces the reported question?
The original verb is future tense; shift it back
Will becomes would in reported speech

prompt: Students can choose ______ to complete a research project ______ to sit a traditional exam at the end of the course.

wrongChoiceReasons.B: Neither...nor creates a double negative meaning, which doesn't fit this choice between two valid options.
wrongChoiceReasons.C: Both...and would mean students must do both tasks, but the sentence presents a choice.
wrongChoiceReasons.D: Not only...but also adds emphasis to two related ideas, but the sentence presents alternatives rather than addition.

explanation: Well done! Either...or presents two alternatives or options side by side in a balanced way.
explanationSteps: The sentence presents two options
Either...or shows a choice between two alternatives
This fits the context of assessment choices
hints: Are the two ideas connected by addition or alternative?
Which correlative conjunction presents alternatives?
Place either before the first option and or before the second

prompt: Complete the sentence: The airline promised better service; ______ , complaints from passengers continued to rise.
explanation: Excellent! However shows a clear contrast between the airline's promise and the reality.
explanationSteps: The first idea: better service was promised
The second idea: complaints continued to rise
However shows contrast between these two ideas
hints: Is the second idea similar to or different from the first?
Use a conjunctive adverb that shows contrast
However is commonly used after a semicolon

prompt: Rewrite using a subordinating conjunction: 'Teenagers want to protect their privacy. They still share personal data on social media.'
answerKey: Although teenagers want to protect their privacy, they still share personal data on social media.
Teenagers still share personal data on social media although they want to protect their privacy.
Even though teenagers want to protect their privacy, they still share personal data on social media.
explanation: Great transformation! Although introduces a contrast between what teenagers want and what they actually do.
explanationSteps: There is a contrast between the two ideas
Use although or even though to show contrast
Place the conjunction at the beginning of the first clause
hints: Is there a contrast between the two sentences?
Which subordinating conjunction shows contrast?
Try starting with Although or Even though

prompt: Correct the connector error: 'The documentary was biased, moreover it sparked an important public debate.'
answerKey: The documentary was biased; however, it sparked an important public debate.
explanation: Nice catch! Moreover adds similar information, but these two ideas are actually in contrast with each other.
explanationSteps: Being biased and sparking debate contrast with each other
Moreover indicates additional similar information
Use however, but, or yet to show contrast instead
hints: Does bias usually lead to positive public debate?
Choose a connector that shows contrast rather than addition
However fits well after a semicolon

prompt: Fill in the two blanks: ______ renewable energy ______ public transport investment is essential if we want to reduce urban pollution.
explanation: Perfect! Both...and emphasises that two factors share equal importance for solving the problem.
explanationSteps: Two subjects share importance
Use both before the first subject
Use and before the second subject
hints: Are both factors needed for the solution?
Which correlative conjunction adds two subjects together?
Place both before the first noun and and before the second

prompt: None of the educational apps ______ completely free of in-app purchases.

wrongChoiceReasons.A: Although none can be singular in formal contexts, in everyday English and with countable plural nouns like apps, are is commonly accepted and natural.
wrongChoiceReasons.C: Were is plural and past tense, but the sentence describes a general present truth.
wrongChoiceReasons.D: Have been is plural and present perfect, but the sentence states a general fact better expressed with the simple present.

explanation: Well chosen! With countable plural nouns, none is often treated as plural in modern English, especially in everyday contexts.
explanationSteps: None of + plural noun often takes a plural verb
Apps is plural and countable
Use are for a general present statement
hints: Is apps singular or plural?
None of + plural noun usually takes a plural verb in everyday English
Think about how the sentence sounds most natural

prompt: Complete the sentence: The quality of online educational videos ______ (be) improving steadily over the last few years.
explanation: Excellent! The subject is quality, which is singular, so the verb must also be singular despite the plural noun videos in between.
explanationSteps: Identify the subject: quality
Of online educational videos is a prepositional phrase, not the subject
Use has been because quality is singular
hints: What is the real subject of the sentence?
Ignore prepositional phrases when choosing the verb form
Quality is singular, so use has rather than have

prompt: Rewrite with correct subject-verb agreement: 'The crew of the cruise ship are preparing for an emergency drill.'
answerKey: The crew of the cruise ship is preparing for an emergency drill.
explanation: Wonderful! Crew is a collective noun that takes a singular verb when the group acts as a single unit.
explanationSteps: Crew is a collective noun
When acting as a unit, collective nouns take singular verbs
Change are to is preparing
hints: Is the crew acting as one unit or as individuals?
Collective nouns usually take singular verbs in standard grammar
Use is for singular subjects

prompt: Correct the subject-verb agreement error: 'The rise of streaming services have changed how we consume media.'
answerKey: The rise of streaming services has changed how we consume media.
explanation: Good eye! Rise is the singular subject, so it needs a singular verb regardless of the plural noun services nearby.
explanationSteps: Identify the subject: rise
Of streaming services is a prepositional phrase
Change have to has for the singular subject
hints: What is the real subject of the sentence?
Ignore prepositional phrases when choosing the verb
Rise is singular, so use has

prompt: Fill in the two blanks: Each of the volunteers ______ received training, and everyone ______ excited to help at the community centre.
explanation: Perfect! Each and everyone are singular indefinite pronouns, so they both need singular verbs.
explanationSteps: Each is a singular indefinite pronoun
Everyone is also singular
Both require singular verbs: has and is
hints: Is each singular or plural?
Is everyone singular or plural?
Both indefinite pronouns need singular verbs
